# **Quantitative Architectures of Gastropod Ontogeny: A Compendium of Specimen-Resolved Shell Datasets**

## **The Accretionary Shell as a Four-Dimensional Biological Archive**

The accretionary biomineralization of the gastropod shell provides an unusually high-fidelity biological and environmental archive. Because the calcifying mantle edge deposits material sequentially along a shifting generative curve, the shell does not remodel its past structural geometry. Rather, it constitutes a frozen, continuous spatial record of its own developmental cascade.1 While traditional malacology, conchology, and early geometric morphometrics have frequently relied upon summary statistics of the adult terminal form—thereby reducing the rich temporal sequence of growth into static endpoint geometries—modern theoretical morphology demands specimen-resolved, time-variant trajectories. A profound understanding of morphological evolution, particularly concerning within-shell growth trajectories, requires data that quantitatively resolves how form changes as the animal grows.  
The identification and retrieval of quantitative, stage-resolved datasets are critical for probing hypotheses related to chirality, sector independence, modularity, and growth program canalization. Such datasets trace a measurable path through theoretical morphospace, allowing researchers to differentiate mathematically between isometric logarithmic expansion, allometric drift, programmatic self-terminating determinate growth, and non-convergent heteromorphy.1 The transition from qualitative taxonomic descriptions to machine-readable, specimen-level numeric tables has been aggressively facilitated by high-resolution micro-computed tomography (micro-CT), the geometric morphometrics of continuous outlines using Fourier transformations, and the digitization of sclerochronological isotopic profiles.3  
This report provides an exhaustive mapping of the specific data shapes required to track gastropod form across growth-time. It identifies the exact digital repositories, institutional curators, mathematical frameworks, and specific specimen-resolved datasets that capture ontogenetic series, trajectory endpoints, time-resolved growth rates, morphospace occupancy, discrete chirality reversals, and developmental perturbation recoveries.

## **Theoretical Morphospaces and the Geometry of Ontogeny**

To move beyond the trajectory of a single specimen, researchers map hundreds of species into theoretical morphospaces to determine which geometric regions are occupied by nature and which are empty. The fundamental architecture of these spaces, and the shape of the data they contain, is governed by the underlying mathematical models used to parameterize the shell.

### **The Raupian Tradition and Its Limitations**

David Raup's foundational fixed-frame model defined shell geometry using four basic parameters: whorl expansion rate (![][image1]), relative position of the generating curve to the coiling axis (![][image2]), rate of helicocone translation (![][image3]), and the aspect ratio of the generating curve (![][image4]).6 Morphospace-occupancy datasets in the Raupian tradition are essentially large matrices of these four variables, recorded for diverse taxa. For decades, these parameters defined the standard language of theoretical morphology.7  
However, modern analysis reveals a significant limitation: Raup’s morphospace does not possess a true Euclidean structure.7 This means the geometric distance between two points in the morphospace does not linearly correlate with the actual biological or shape difference between the organisms.7 Furthermore, classical applications of Raup's model often assumed a constant set of parameters throughout the life of the organism, imposing an assumption of perfect self-similarity (isometric growth) that rarely holds true in nature.1

### **The Unification of Fixed and Moving Frames**

Recent theoretical syntheses, notably advanced in the preprints of Ido Filin (2025/2026), have sought to reconcile these models by identifying unifying geometric parameters. Filin's work focuses on the "lead angle" (the defining slope of a conical helix) as a parameter that bridges fixed-frame models (like Raup's) and moving-frame models.8 By extracting centerline spirals from aggregate historical datasets—combining the work of Schindel, Collins, Noshita, and Larsson—Filin generated a harmonized dataset (available via Zenodo: DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19919684 and 10.5281/zenodo.20073984) that probes the covariation of coiling parameters.3  
The data confirms that the centerline spirals of gastropod shells are well-approximated by isometrically growing conical logspirals, while true ontogenetic allometry manifests strictly in the relative growth and shifting shape of the apertures.11 Furthermore, this synthesized dataset proves that the inverse relation frequently observed between spire-height and expansion rate—often attributed strictly to ecological or mechanical adaptation—can equally be explained by mathematically mandated covariation dictated by taxon-specific lead angles.3 This dataset allows researchers to disentangle true biological allometry from mathematically forced constraints.  
Modern successors to the Raupian tradition have also mapped these parameters to landmark-based geometric morphometrics or fractal dimension analyses to bypass the subjectivity of manual landmarking. For example, researchers investigating Viviparidae gastropods in Lake Lugu generated datasets combining linear analysis, traditional landmarks, and novel fractal dimension values extracted from 3D scans to quantify the roughness of the shell surface, effectively capturing micro-sculpture variations alongside gross coiling geometry.4 Such unified datasets are typically hosted on the Dryad Digital Repository.

| Theoretical Framework | Key Parameters | Model Type | Primary Use Case in Extant Datasets |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Raup (1966)** | **![][image5]** | Fixed-frame Cartesian/Cylindrical | Broad macroevolutionary morphospace plotting; steady-state adult approximations. |
| **Okamoto (1988)** | **![][image6]** | Moving-frame (Frenet) | Heteromorphy; irregular uncoiling; ontogenetic trajectories lacking a central axis. |
| **Collins et al. (2021)** | **![][image7]** | Empirical relative angle (![][image8]) | High-resolution ontogenetic allometry; continuous trajectory mapping. |
| **Filin (2025)** | Lead angle, Slant | Conical logspiral / Helix | Reconciling fixed and moving frames; isolating aperture allometry from centerline isometric growth. |

## **The Pinnacle of Trajectory Data: Ontogenetic Series**

The highest-value data shape for tracking within-shell growth trajectories consists of ontogenetic series: sequential shape measurements taken at successive growth stages of the exact same individual shell. Rather than comparing the adult species means of different taxa, these datasets trace the developmental path continuously from the protoconch to the terminal aperture, capturing the localized allometries and dynamic parameter shifts that occur during the lifespan.

### **The Collins et al. Helicospiral Dataset**

The single richest and most rigorously quantified existing dataset for studying how shell form evolves along growth-time is the empirical morphospace dataset compiled by Collins, Klapaukh, Crampton, et al. (2021).1 Associated with the study "Going round the twist—an empirical analysis of shell coiling in helicospiral gastropods," this repository provides an unprecedented look at ontogenetic sequences of apertures extracted from sectioned specimens and microtomography.3  
The Collins dataset encompasses 176 fossil and modern gastropod shells, spanning 25 distinct species, with high-resolution, specimen-level measurements taken across successive growth stages.1 The raw data matrices and associated R routines for calculating helicospiral parameters are archived in machine-readable formats on the Dryad Digital Repository and Zenodo (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.12159344).3  
Crucially, this dataset eschews the assumption of isometric growth (the constant logarithmic spiral) that has dominated theoretical morphology.1 Instead, it tracks the empirical, localized rates of change for four key parameters across the ontogenetic trajectory:

* ![][image9]: The empirical rate of whorl expansion, denoting the rate of aperture area increase.1  
* ![][image10]: The empirical rate of downward translation or growth along the coiling axis.1  
* ![][image11]: The empirical rate of movement of the aperture away from the central coiling axis.1  
* ![][image12]: The empirical rate of change of the shape of the aperture, derived precisely from a fast Fourier transform (FFT) of the aperture coordinates.1

By measuring the angle ![][image8] around the coiling axis from the *preceding* aperture, the Collins dataset elegantly bypasses a major methodological pitfall known as the "arbitrary datum" problem.1 The apex or protoconch of a shell is almost always lost, eroded, or too small to measure accurately. By calculating translation and expansion relative to the instantaneous ![][image8] angle rather than a fixed global origin (![][image13]), the ontogenetic trajectory is quantified seamlessly regardless of the missing apical data.1 The findings embedded in this dataset conclusively demonstrate that the assumption of isometric, self-similar growth fails across the examined taxa, revealing that the ontogenetic path of a gastropod is characterized by continuous, deliberate allometric modulation.1

### **Legacy Datasets and Synthetic Compilations**

Beyond the Collins repository, several foundational ontogenetic datasets of the late 20th and early 21st centuries have been digitized, augmented, and integrated into modern morphometric analyses.

1. **Schindel (1990) Compilations:** Schindel’s foundational tables contain estimates of coiling parameters for hundreds of species and specimens of marine, freshwater, and terrestrial gastropods. These tables capture whorl expansion rates, apical angles, and longitudinal exponential expansion rates.3 Though originally older analog data, these tables have been digitized and incorporated into modern Dryad and Zenodo repositories by subsequent authors like Noshita and Filin.3  
2. **Noshita et al. (2012) Functional Constraints Data:** This dataset mapped the theoretical morphospace of aperture inclination (![][image14], representing vertical inclination, and ![][image15], representing horizontal inclination) against Raupian coiling geometry.6 The dataset demonstrates how functional constraints, such as the ability to maintain balance or adhere to substrates, limit theoretical occupancy. The underlying numeric tables, measured from lateral and umbilical specimen views, are fully archived on Dryad (DOI: 10.5061/dryad.6mk023c0).3  
3. **Larsson et al. (2020) Intraspecific Allometry:** Focusing on the intertidal marine snail *Littorina saxatilis*, this dataset tracks continuous centerline spirals and provides direct estimates for intraspecific variation and ontogenetic allometry, capturing how local environmental pressures modulate the growth trajectory within a single species.3

These pooled datasets, actively curated by researchers spanning empirical paleobiology to theoretical developmental biology, represent the current frontier for extracting normalized, stage-resolved mathematical parameters across broad taxonomic scales.3

## **Trajectory Endpoints: Heteromorphy and Determinate Cessation**

When analyzing the within-shell growth trajectory as a recorded morphogenetic cascade, the two most critical endpoints are the failure to converge on a self-similar spiral (heteromorphy) and the highly regulated, programmatic arrival at zero growth (determinate cessation). Datasets that quantify these endpoints are prioritized because they probe the absolute limits, stability, and eventual termination of the underlying developmental program.

### **Non-Convergent Growth and Heteromorphy**

Heteromorphy represents a gross departure from the self-similar logarithmic spiral, resulting in irregular, uncoiling, meandering, or scalariform growth patterns.17 To quantify this departure as a continuous mathematical trajectory rather than merely classifying it as a qualitative anomaly, moving-frame differential geometry is strictly required.  
The definitive mathematical framework for this is Takashi Okamoto's "growing tube model" (1988), which elegantly bypasses the need for a fixed central coiling axis.18 Instead, the model constructs the shell tube through the accretionary growth of the aperture using a local, moving Frenet frame.18 Because the coordinate system continuously travels with the growing aperture, the model perfectly accommodates shells that never converge to a spiral, or those that converge and subsequently deviate radically into retroversal hooks or straight shafts.18 Under this model, the trajectory is precisely quantified by three differential parameters:

* ![][image16]: Radius enlarging ratio (representing the expansion of the tube).  
* ![][image17]: Standardized curvature (representing the bending of the tube).  
* ![][image3]: Standardized torsion (representing the twisting of the tube).18

**Key Datasets and Code Repositories for Heteromorphy:**  
Machine-readable data for heteromorph trajectories exists primarily in two paired forms: raw point-cloud/CT coordinate data of heteromorph specimens, and the algorithmically extracted ![][image6] computational parameters.

1. **Misaki, Okamoto, and Maeda (2023) Dataset:** This dataset quantitatively maps the evolutionary process of extremely twisted heteromorph ammonites (such as *Pravitoceras sigmoidale*, *Didymoceras awajiense*, and *D. morozumii*) from the Upper Cretaceous.19 Housed on Dryad (DOI: 10.5061/dryad.q573n5tnz), the dataset includes Data\_2\_Parameter\_values\_of\_the\_growing\_tube\_model\_for\_each\_morphotype.csv.20 This tabular data specifically quantifies the shift between morphotypes characterized by uncoiled shafts and retroversal hooks, tracking the exact point in the ontogenetic trajectory where the coiling pattern deviates from a standard spiral, proving there are no significant geometric gaps in the evolutionary continuum of these lineages.19  
2. **Noshita's Estimation Algorithms:** The computational code required to fit the growing tube model to empirical 3D shell trajectories and extract the differential ![][image18], and ![][image3] parameters is actively maintained on GitHub (noshita/growing\_tube\_model\_estimation).21 Written in Python, this repository acts as the standard bioinformatic tool for converting micro-CT segmentation meshes of irregular or malformed shells into numeric parameter arrays, enabling direct quantitative comparison between wild-type and aberrant growth trajectories.22  
3. **Buoyancy and Life Orientation Simulations:** The parameters extracted via the growing tube model have been historically combined with Trueman's buoyancy equations to generate datasets mapping the ontogenetic change in life orientation.24 For example, simulations of the hamitoid shell of *Polyptychoceras pseudogaultinum* suggest that changes in the absolute growth rate were directly dependent on the living attitude of the ammonite in the water column—growing slowly when the aperture faced upward and rapidly when facing other directions.17

### **Determinate Growth Run to Cessation**

Determinate growth in gastropods is defined as the programmatic deceleration and absolute termination of spiral translation.2 This cessation is almost universally marked by terminal apertural elaborations—such as the pronounced thickening, flaring, or reflection of the final outer lip, the downward descent of the suture, or the excavation of the inner lip.25 Datasets capturing this phenomenon are crucial for determining whether growth stops at a characteristic, energetically predictable point, or if it is purely a function of chronological age.  
In indeterminate growers, the trajectory of shape relative to body size shows a gradual, asymptotic decline in the energy converted to somatic growth post-maturity, modeled accurately by von Bertalanffy growth curves.2 In determinate growers, however, the trajectory hits a hard, structural asymptote. The terminal lip flare represents the biological sink for calcium carbonate once spiral growth permanently ceases, indicating a massive shift in physiological energetics toward reproduction and defense.2  
**Key Datasets for Determinate Cessation:**

1. **Queen Conch (*Aliger gigas* / *Strombus gigas*) Lip Thickness Databases:** The Queen conch serves as the premier model organism for determinate growth because its terminal lip thickness is used as a direct, quantitative proxy for sexual maturity and time-since-growth-cessation.29 Large-scale, specimen-resolved datasets mapping spiral shell length against apertural lip thickness are maintained by the U.S. Federal Government and NOAA.31 Specifically, the NCEI Accession 0253469 ("Radial survey data of queen conch population counts, shell length, and lip thickness measurements") contains georeferenced, quantitative measurements of the arrival at growth stop across thousands of individuals.32 These extensive CSV datasets correlate the precise cessation point—quantified as LT50 (lip thickness at 50% population maturity, typically ranging from 4 to 14 mm depending on sex and geography)—with external environmental variables.29  
2. **Size-at-Maturity and Phenotypic Plasticity Meta-Analyses:** Datasets tracking the extreme variability of the cessation point exist for intertidal species like *Amphissa columbiana*, which exhibits profound phenotypic plasticity. Depending on environmental pressures, individuals develop either as rapidly growing thin-lipped morphs or as thick-lipped morphs that have undergone determinate cessation.27 Corresponding ecological interaction datasets involving such species are highly structured and available on Dryad (e.g., DOI: 10.5061/dryad.8m11n).35  
3. **Deep-Sea Lineage Matrices (Gazidae and Rapaninae):** Phylogenetic and morphological character matrices tracking the deep-time evolution of the terminal lip, inner-lip excavation, and apertural descending trajectories exist for specific siphonate caenogastropod subfamilies like Rapaninae and Gazidae.25 These datasets, which often take the form of discrete character matrices embedded in supplementary Nexus or .tps files, chart how determinate growth evolved independently multiple times, particularly during the Neogene, potentially as a functional adaptation against escalating shell-peeling predation.26

The synthesis of these determinate growth datasets suggests that the cessation point is not merely a passive function of chronological age. Instead, it represents an active energetic threshold where the metabolic cost of further spiral translation outweighs the reproductive benefits, triggering the deployment of a localized, highly canalized "countdown" morphogenetic program that irrevocably seals the aperture.27

## **Sclerochronology: Time-Resolved Growth Rates**

While geometric morphometrics provide the precise spatial trajectory of ontogeny, sclerochronology provides the essential temporal derivative: the actual rate of biomineralization resolved over calendar time. By microscopically measuring the width of daily, tidal, or seasonal growth increments—and coupling these physical measurements with sequential stable isotope ratios (![][image19], ![][image20], ![][image21])—sclerochronologists reconstruct highly precise time-series of exactly "how fast the shell was building" against known environmental drivers.5

### **The Bivalve Bias and the Geometric Challenge of Gastropods**

A critical statistical insight regarding the landscape of sclerochronological archives is the severe taxonomic bias toward bivalves. A systematic review of freshwater sclerochronology publications reveals that an overwhelming 81% of studies analyze exclusively bivalve taxa, while only 14% focus solely on gastropods.37 This disparity exists despite gastropods comprising the vast majority of freshwater molluscan species diversity.37  
The underrepresentation of gastropods is primarily a geometric and methodological problem. The tightly coiled, helicospiral nature of the gastropod shell makes continuous, sequential micromilling along the axis of growth technically arduous. Unlike the relatively planar growth axis of a bivalve hinge or flat outer shell layer, navigating a drill along a 3D gastropod whorl while avoiding cross-contamination of adjacent temporal layers requires immense precision. Consequently, long, continuous isotopic time-series of gastropod growth rates are exceptionally rare, highly valuable, and actively sought by the paleoclimatological community.

### **Major Repositories and Extant Gastropod Datasets**

The undisputed central repository for quantitative sclerochronological and paleoclimate-proxy datasets is **PANGAEA** (Data Publisher for Earth & Environmental Science).38 PANGAEA hosts raw numeric tables of isotopic sequences measured strictly *along* the ontogenetic growth paths of individual shells, making it the premier archive for time-resolved trajectories.  
**The Palmer et al. (2021) Database (PANGAEA.941373):** The single richest aggregated database for time-resolved isotopic trajectories in nearshore environments is the Holocene nearshore marine mollusk shell geochemistry database compiled by Palmer et al..39

* **Scope and Range:** It encompasses 598 modern, archaeological, and sub-fossil shells spanning from 8,880 years Before Present (BP) to the modern day, sourced from the California Current System.39  
* **Resolution:** It contains a staggering 4,917 total ![][image20] and 7,366 total ![][image19] stage-resolved individual measurements.41  
* **Data Shape:** The data is highly specimen-resolved, featuring between 1 and 345 sequential samples drilled per individual shell (averaging 44.7 samples per trajectory). This allows researchers to track the seasonal oscillations in temperature and internal growth rate throughout the entirety of the animal's life.39 While bivalves like *Mytilus* dominate the dataset, it includes vital, high-resolution gastropod representation suitable for trajectory analysis.41

**Other Highly Resolved Gastropod Trajectories:**

* **Tibetan Plateau *Radix* sp. Dataset (PANGAEA.777838):** Compiled by Heyde, Slotta, and colleagues, this dataset contains sub-seasonal, high-resolution ![][image19] and ![][image20] profiles of the aquatic gastropod *Radix*.43 The sequential isotope patterns clearly trace interannual monsoon variability, precipitation levels, and evaporative forces across the animal's ontogeny, essentially functioning as a continuous hydrologic data logger.43  
* ***Littorina littorea*** **Seasonal Profiles:** Datasets tracking the ontogenetic ![][image19] profiles from the apertural margins of *Littorina littorea* (the common periwinkle) derived from the Skaill Farm archaeological site provide high-resolution calendar-time mapping of growth rates.44 Because the timing and rate of seasonal shell growth in these specimens remain constant through their lifetime, the dataset perfectly resolves the environmental conditions experienced right up to the cessation point or harvest, distinguishing between winter shutdowns and summer growth spurts.44 Similar highly resolved data exists for the species *Gibbula cineraria* in the North Sea.46  
* **St. Joseph Bay *Busycon sinistrum* Sclerochronology:** Isotope profiling on lightning whelks (*Busycon sinistrum*) maps the ![][image19] and ![][image20] trajectories to reconstruct the seasonality of foraging by late prehistoric cultures, capturing the precise environmental chemistry along the spiral path of these large predatory gastropods.47

The integration of PANGAEA's isotopic temporal time-series with Dryad's 3D spatial trajectories represents the absolute frontier of gastropod theoretical morphology, allowing form, spatial orientation, and physical growth-time to be modeled simultaneously as a unified dynamic system.

## **Discrete-State Flips: Chirality Reversals and Introgressive Genetics**

While growth rates, morphospace positions, and allometric expansions are continuous, floating-point variables, chirality (handedness) is a discrete, binary state (dextral or sinistral). Reversals in chirality—either as teratological aberrations in individuals or as stable evolutionary lineages that flipped hand entirely—are exceptionally rare, high-value events. They represent a fundamental inversion of the morphogenetic coordinate system. Datasets that track the inheritance pedigrees, the genomic sequencing, and the physical cytoskeletal onset of these reversals pinpoint the exact molecular moments the discrete state permanently changes.

### ***Lsdia1*** **and the Cytoskeletal Pivot at Third Cleavage**

In the model pond snail *Lymnaea stagnalis*, chirality is determined by a single maternal locus acting at the very early embryonic stage.48 The breakthrough identification of this locus points to *Lsdia1*, a tandemly duplicated diaphanous-related formin gene.50 A naturally occurring disabling frame-shift mutation in one copy of this gene perfectly associates with symmetry breaking, effectively converting dextral snails to a sinistral phenocopy.50  
The physical manifestation of this discrete state change occurs at the exact moment of the third cleavage (the 8-cell stage of the embryo). Quantitative datasets tracking the cytoskeletal dynamics—using fluorescently labeled filamentous actin and microtubules—demonstrate that the spiral deformation and spindle inclination present in dominant dextral embryos are completely absent in sinistral mutants.48 The genomic BAC assemblies, transcriptome profiles spanning the central nervous system, and specific formin intron sequence alignments for *Lymnaea*, *Euhadra*, and *Partula* are fully machine-readable and accessible on Dryad (DOI: 10.5061/dryad.r4342) and the NCBI Sequence Read Archive.50

### **Breeding Matrices and Introgressive Gene Flow**

To understand how discrete chirality flips propagate, establish new lineages, or extinguish in the wild, researchers rely on extensive multi-generational breeding records. Because sinistrality can be a recessive trait, up to three generations of controlled breeding may be required to bring homozygous sinistral alleles together in a single individual.54

* ***Euhadra*** **and *Partula* Datasets:** Researchers such as Angus Davison, Bryan Clarke, and colleagues have curated massive datasets spanning decades on the fecundity, viability, and cross-breeding compatibility of different chiral morphs of *Partula suturalis*, *Lymnaea stagnalis*, and Japanese *Euhadra* species.55 Surprisingly, these datasets provide empirical, sequence-backed evidence of mating and ongoing genetic introgression between mirror-image morphs (dextral and sinistral) of *Euhadra* in the wild.55  
  * *Insight:* This specific dataset profoundly shifts theoretical morphology and speciation theory. A chiral flip creates a severe mechanical mating barrier (as the genitalia are on opposite sides of the body), but the data proves it is a *semi-permeable* barrier. This implies that a discrete state change in chirality alone is insufficient to guarantee complete reproductive isolation and immediate speciation.55 The underlying RAD-seq phylogenomic data, single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) matrices, and maximum likelihood phylogenies charting this gene flow are hosted on Dryad.55  
* ***Cornu aspersum*** **(Jeremy the Snail) Pedigrees:** The high-profile citizen science project centered on "Jeremy," a remarkably rare sinistral common garden snail (*Cornu aspersum*), resulted in a comprehensive breeding pedigree and genomic sequencing dataset.58 The full reference genome sequence and the associated multi-generational inheritance records are archived in open-access repositories (Wellcome Open Research / European Nucleotide Archive), providing a pristine, modern dataset for tracking the inheritance and population dynamics of teratological left-handedness.58

## **Perturbation, Resilience, and Morphological Recovery**

The structural resilience and canalization of an ontogenetic trajectory are best tested when the organism is physically damaged or chemically disrupted. Perturbation datasets measure a shell's form prior to an injury or molecular shock, track the immediate structural malformation as a mathematical deviation, and precisely quantify the trajectory's convergence back to the original baseline (or its failure to do so).

### **Chemical Perturbation: The Wnt Pathway and the Modularity of Growth**

Recent experimental datasets have utilized the Wnt signaling pathway activator (BIO) to artificially perturb the early embryonic development of freshwater gastropods (*Pseudosuccinea columella* and *Biomphalaria glabrata*).22 The resulting malformations are highly specific, and rather than being assessed qualitatively, they are quantitatively tracked using 3D CT reconstructions and the extraction of parameters via Okamoto's growing tube model.22  
The quantitative data reveals two distinct malformation trajectories resulting from Wnt activation:

1. **Cap-shaped (Cap):** Characterized by an almost total loss of coiling, resulting in a limpet-like morphology. The theoretical morphology parameters reveal a massive, statistically significant increase in the Expansion (![][image16]) parameter, mathematically confirming an unregulated, rapid widening of the aperture.22  
2. **Compressed (Com):** Characterized by a reduced number of coils and a compressed spire. The extracted parameters show significantly elevated Expansion (![][image16]) and Torsion (![][image3]), while the Curvature (![][image17]) parameter remains strictly unaffected and tied identically to the control baseline.22

*Insight into Sector Independence:* This dataset provides powerful empirical proof of modularity and sector independence within the gastropod growth program. Because the chemical activation of Wnt alters apertural widening (![][image16]) and twisting (![][image3]) without affecting the baseline bending trajectory (![][image17]), it demonstrates that the different mathematical axes of the morphogenetic coordinate system are under decoupled genetic control.22 The raw 3D mesh datasets and the exact Python algorithms used to extract these ![][image16], ![][image17], and ![][image3] parameters from the perturbed embryos are openly accessible on GitHub (noshita/growing\_tube\_model\_estimation).23

### **Physical Damage, "Road-Holding", and Repair**

Datasets tracking physical shell repair frequencies offer insights into the "road-holding" capacity of the mantle—its ability to re-establish the proper coiling trajectory after a catastrophic break.60 Checa's theoretical models of regulation in *Sphincterochila* posit that the shell tube possesses an unmineralized region lined by a flexible periostracal sheet, allowing the organism to use tactile feedback from the previous whorl to guide the future trajectory.3

* **Trace Malformation Data:** Datasets tracking the quantification of bioerosional traces, predator-induced breaks, and subsequently repaired malformations (e.g., in *Zidona dufresnii*) are available on Dryad (DOI: 10.5061/dryad.j3tx95xwg) in tabular .csv formats mapping shell dimensions against repair scars.62  
* **Repair Frequency Matrices:** Broader ecological datasets (such as those by Stafford et al.) tabulate the precise proportion of specimens bearing repair scars relative to shell thickness, shell height, and geographic locality.63 While morphometrically simpler than full 3D recovery trajectories, these numeric tables correlate environmental predation pressure with the incidence of morphogenetic recovery, proving that the ontogenetic trajectory is highly robust to physical truncation.  
* **Epizoan Obstruction:** In ammonite models, fabricational defects caused by epizoan attachment force the growing tube to deviate into trochospiral patterns to maintain growth direction. The quantitative modeling of these "natural experiments" tracks how the aperture reacts to temporary trajectory shifts before returning to a planispiral baseline, further supporting the road-holding hypothesis.60

## **Data Infrastructure, Digitization Formats, and Synthesis**

The landscape of gastropod morphological data is rapidly transitioning from isolated supplementary PDFs and private laboratory spreadsheets into unified, machine-readable digital infrastructure. Understanding where this data is stored, the standardized formats it utilizes, and the primary institutional curators is critical for any multi-study meta-analysis.

### **Active Repositories and Curators**

1. **Dryad and Zenodo:** These two linked repositories host the overwhelming majority of modern, 3D, and parameterized spatial shape data. Dryad is the primary host for the Collins et al. helicospiral trajectories 3, the Noshita morphospace functional constraints 3, the Misaki heteromorph ammonite models 20, the Tatoosh intertidal interaction matrices 35, and the Davison chirality genomic arrays.55 Zenodo frequently mirrors these datasets or hosts updated code bases and preprints (such as Filin's comprehensive logspiral compilations).10  
2. **PANGAEA:** This is the exclusive domain for high-resolution, calendar-time resolved sclerochronological arrays. It houses the massive Palmer et al. Holocene database 39 and the Tibetan Plateau *Radix* trajectories.38  
3. **NOAA NCEI & Data.gov:** These platforms act as the central archives for massive population-level biometric trajectories relevant to fisheries management and determinate growth, particularly the *Aliger gigas* lip thickness and radial survey records.31  
4. **MorphoSource:** While historically heavily utilized for vertebrate morphology, MorphoSource is increasingly hosting the raw micro-CT meshes of gastropods from which parameters are computationally extracted.

### **Standardization and Formats**

The realization of a fully poolable, multi-study gastropod dataset relies on three standard digitization formats currently in widespread use:

* **CT Meshes (.stl, .ply):** The raw 3D point-cloud data capturing the entire exterior shell volume and internal spatial septa.4  
* **Landmark Configurations (.tps):** Thin-plate spline formats used natively by geometric morphometric software like tpsDig2 to record 2D/3D coordinate arrays of homologous points or sliding semi-landmarks continuously along the aperture.64  
* **Parameter Matrices (.csv):** The final extracted trajectories—whether ![][image22] derived from Collins 1, or ![][image6] derived from Okamoto 18—stored as simple comma-separated variables tracking parameter values sequentially against whorl number or rotation angle ![][image8].1

The active laboratories and researchers most likely to hold unaggregated but digitized trajectory series include the networks surrounding Katie Collins and James Crampton (empirical shell coiling) 1, Takashi Okamoto and Koji Noshita (theoretical growing tube and moving frames) 18, Angus Davison (chirality, breeding matrices, and genomics) 59, and Bernd Schöne (high-resolution sclerochronology).36

## **Conclusion**

The analytical pursuit of the within-shell growth trajectory has successfully moved beyond the static measurement of adult terminal forms. For researchers seeking numeric, specimen-resolved datasets that map exactly how biological form changes over time, the landscape is deep, heavily quantified, and stored across discrete, specialized repositories.  
The single richest dataset for spatial ontogeny is undoubtedly the **Collins et al. (2021) empirical helicospiral dataset** on Dryad, which meticulously tracks apertural translation and expansion parameters, proving the continuous allometric nature of gastropod growth without relying on arbitrary apical datums.1 For modeling the extreme endpoints of these trajectories—specifically non-convergent heteromorphy and perturbation recoveries—**Okamoto’s growing tube model**, deployed alongside Noshita's open-source Python parameter-extraction algorithms on GitHub, represents the premier mathematical and computational standard.18 To understand determinate growth cessation, the massive **NOAA *Aliger gigas* lip-thickness surveys** provide thousands of datapoints linking morphological stops to energetic maturity.32 Finally, to anchor these spatial transformations to absolute calendar time, the **Palmer et al. (2021) PANGAEA database** offers the most exhaustive isotopic arrays of accretionary growth rates available.39  
The true synthesis of this field will emerge when the discrete genetic switches of chirality (such as the *Lsdia1* locus), the spatial moving-frames of theoretical morphology, and the isotopic time-series of sclerochronology are mapped simultaneously onto the exact same specimen cohorts. Only then will the gastropod shell be fully decoded as a unified, four-dimensional biological archive.

#### **Works cited**

1.
